content_blocks.text:
56 
chapter 3
there are no
Dumb Questions
Q:How big is a reference 
variable?
A:You don’t know. Unless 
you’re cozy with someone on the 
JVM’5 development team, you 
don’t know how a reference is 
represented. There are pointers 
in there somewhere, but you 
can’t access them. You won’t 
need to. (OK, if you insist, you 
might as well just imagine it 
to be a 64-bit value.) But when 
you’re talking about memory 
allocation issues, your Big 
Concern should be about how 
many objects (as opposed to 
object references) you’re creating, 
and how big they (the objects) 
really are.
Q:50, does that mean that 
all object references are the 
same size, regardless of the size 
of the actual objects to which 
they refer?
A:Yep. All references for a 
given JVM will be the same 
size regardless of the objects 
they reference, but each JVM 
might have a different way of 
representing references, 50 
references on one JVM may be 
smaller or larger than references 
on another JVM.
Q:Can 1 do arithmetic on a 
reference variable, increment 
it, you know – C stuff?
A:Nope. Say it with me again, 
“Java is not C.” 
object references 
HeadFirst: 50, tell us, what’5 life like for an object reference? 
Reference: Pretty simple, really. 1’m a remote control and 1 can be programmed to 
control different objects.
HeadFirst: Do you mean different objects even while you’re running? Like, can you 
refer to a Dog and then five minutes later refer to a Car?
Reference: Of course not. Once 1’m declared, that’5 it. If 1’m a Dog remote control 
then 1’11 never be able to point (oops – my bad, we’re not supposed to say point) 1 mean 
refer to anything but a Dog.
HeadFirst: Does that mean you can refer to only one Dog?
Reference: No. 1 can be referring to one Dog, and then five minutes later 1 can refer to 
some other Dog. As long as it’5 a Dog, 1 can be redirected (like reprogramming your remote 
to a different TV) to it. Unless... no never mind. 
HeadFirst: No, tell me. What were you gonna say?
Reference: 1 don’t think you want to get into this now, but 1’11 just give you the short 
version – if 1’m marked as final, then once 1 am assigned a Dog, 1 can never be repro-
grammed to anything else but that one and only Dog. In other words, no other object can 
be assigned to me. 
HeadFirst: You’re right, we don’t want to talk about that now. OK, 50 unless you’re 
final, then you can refer to one Dog and then refer to a different Dog later. Can you ever 
refer to nothing at all? 15 it possible to not be programmed to anything?
Reference: Yes, but it disturbs me to talk about it.
HeadFirst: Why is that? 
Reference: Because it means 1’m null, and that’5 upsetting to me. 
HeadFirst: You mean, because then you have no value?
Reference: Oh, null is a value. 1’m still a remote control, but it’5 like you brought 
home a new universal remote control and you don’t have a TV. 1’m not programmed to 
control anything. They can press my buttons all day long, but nothing good happens. 1 
just feel 50... useless. A waste of bits. Granted, not that many bits, but still. And that’5 not 
the worst part. If 1 am the only reference to a particular object, and then 1’m set to null 
(deprogrammed), it means that now nobody can get to that object 1 had been referring to.
HeadFirst: And that’5 bad because...
Reference: You have to ask? Here 1’ve developed a relationship with this object, an 
intimate connection, and then the tie is suddenly, cruelly, severed. And 1 will never see 
that object again, because now it’5 eligible for [producer, cue tragic music] garbage collection. 
Sniff. But do you think programmers ever consider that? Snif. Why, why can’t 1 be a primi-
tive? 1 hate being a reference. The responsibility, all the broken attachments...
This week’5 interview:
Object Reference
Java Exposed
